name: El Destierro Route
name_original: Quintanas de Gormaz desde Berlanga de Duero — circular por el Camino del Cid Cicloturista
description: Cycle the El Destierro Route, a 20.0-mile (32.2 km) moderate touring cycling route rich in history and scenic landscapes.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Berlanga de Duero traverse a landscape characterized by rolling hills, forests of oak, pine, and juniper, and the expansive Castilian plateau. The Escalote River, a tributary of the Duero, winds through the area, providing natural features. The region is situated at the northeastern foothills of the Ayllón mountain range, offering varied terrain for cyclists. Best touring cycling routes around Berlanga De Duero The most popular touring cycling route is El Destierro Route, a 20.0 miles (32.2 km) trail that takes 1 hour 56 minutes to complete. This route offers a journey through the historical landscapes of the region. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Berlanga Castle – Bordecorex loop from Berlanga de Duero, a moderate 19.6 miles (31.5 km) path. This route explores the area around the historic Berlanga Castle and the Castilian plateau. Local touring cyclists also love the Berlanga Castle – Berlanga de Duero loop from Berlanga de Duero, a 7.7 miles (12.3 km) trail leading through the town and past the castle, often completed in about 45 minutes. Touring cycling around Berlanga De Duero is defined by rolling hills, historical sites like castles, and river valleys. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and challenging routes suitable for different ability levels. The routes in Berlanga De Duero are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.25 stars from over 13 reviews. More than 69 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Berlanga De Duero's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Berlanga de Duero?
A: There are over 10 touring cycling routes around Berlanga de Duero, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.25 stars from over 13 reviews.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Berlanga de Duero?
A: The region is characterized by rolling hills, vast expanses of the Castilian plateau, and river valleys, particularly along the Escalote River. You'll find a mix of mostly paved surfaces, with some unpaved segments, especially on routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38181016" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Berlanga de Duero – Nufri Apple Orchards, La Rasa loop from El Jabinar&lt;/a&gt;, which features significant elevation changes.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, there are routes suitable for varying fitness levels. An easy option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20142363" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Berlanga Castle – Berlanga de Duero loop from Berlanga de Duero&lt;/a&gt;, which is about 7.7 miles (12.3 km) long and takes approximately 45 minutes to complete, leading through the town and past the historic castle.

Q: What are some challenging touring cycling routes in the area?
A: For a more challenging experience, consider routes with significant elevation gain. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39558268" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de la Carrascosa – Berlanga de Duero loop from Lumías&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult route covering over 96 km with more than 1200 meters of ascent, offering panoramic views of the Soria plateau.

Q: What historical landmarks or natural features can I see along the cycling routes?
A: Cycling routes often pass by significant historical sites and natural beauty. You can explore the imposing &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5619297" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Berlanga Castle&lt;/a&gt;, the traditional &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2701891" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza Mayor of Berlanga de Duero&lt;/a&gt;, or the majestic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2054152" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gormaz Castle&lt;/a&gt;, recognized as Europe's largest defensive construction of the Late Middle Ages. Natural features include the winding Escalote River and the scenic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2615903" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de la Carrascosa&lt;/a&gt; mountain pass.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes around Berlanga de Duero?
A: Yes, many routes are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31451329" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Berlanga Castle – Bordecorex loop from Berlanga de Duero&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20142363" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Berlanga Castle – Berlanga de Duero loop from Berlanga de Duero&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: When is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Berlanga de Duero?
A: Due to its continental Mediterranean climate with cool winters, spring and autumn are ideal for touring cycling in Berlanga de Duero. The weather is pleasant, and the scenery is vibrant, making for comfortable rides.

Q: Can I find cafes or accommodation near the cycling routes in Berlanga de Duero?
A: Berlanga de Duero, as a historical municipality, offers amenities within the town itself, including cafes and accommodation. Many routes start or pass through the town, making it convenient to access these services before or after your ride.

Q: Is there public transport or parking available for cyclists in Berlanga de Duero?
A: While specific public transport options for cyclists might be limited, Berlanga de Duero is accessible by road. Parking is generally available within the town, especially near central areas, providing convenient starting points for your cycling adventures.

Q: What kind of wildlife can I expect to see while cycling in the region?
A: The area around Berlanga de Duero is part of important environmental and wildlife reserves, including the Place of Community Interest and Special Bird Protection Area known as Altos de Barahona. You might spot various bird species such as eagles, vultures, and storks soaring overhead, particularly along the Escalote River.

Q: What is the average difficulty level of touring cycling routes in Berlanga de Duero?
A: The routes around Berlanga de Duero offer a balanced mix of difficulty levels. Out of the 11 available routes, 1 is easy, 6 are moderate, and 4 are considered difficult, ensuring options for cyclists of all abilities.
tips: Berlanga de Duero is a small town in the northern Spanish province of Soria whose history most likely dates back to pre-Roman times, when there is said to have been a Celtic settlement here. Of course, the castle ruins enthroned above the village are particularly impressive and worth seeing.

The church of San Martín, in Aguilera, is a Romanesque church from the middle of the 12th century. The large dimensions of the church stand out, as well as its beautiful porticoed gallery in ashlar masonry.

I met her on the Camino del Cid that I did in 2019 and I thought she was beautiful. 
We stopped for breakfast in the square right in front, and we stocked up on some good empanadas in a bakery on the corner right next door. 

As a curious note, inside the church there is a lizard that Friar Tomás de Berlanga brought from Panama.

faqs.question: What is the historical significance of El Destierro Route?
faqs.answer: The route is deeply connected to the epic Spanish poem "Cantar de Mio Cid," following the itinerary of El Cid's banishment from Castile. It's rich in history, passing through numerous castles, watchtowers, and monasteries that played roles in medieval Spain.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for cycling on this route?
faqs.answer: The El Destierro Route offers two main variants. The road cycling route is predominantly on asphalted roads with light traffic, suitable for touring bikes, though avoiding narrow tires is advised for slightly uneven sections. The gravel/MTB route consists of dirt tracks, narrow tarmac roads, and some easy trails. Some segments may be unpaved and difficult, potentially requiring you to push your bike, but alternative MTB trails are signposted for tougher sections.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of El Destierro Route for cycling?
faqs.answer: The road cycling route is generally considered to have low difficulty with few significant climbs, making it suitable for various skill levels. The gravel/MTB route requires good to very good fitness, with some segments classified as "hard gravel ride" due to unpaved and difficult sections and potentially significant elevation gains.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle El Destierro Route?
faqs.answer: The best seasons to cycle this route are spring and autumn. These months offer milder temperatures, allowing you to avoid the high heat of summer and the cold and rain of winter.

faqs.question: Are there any notable points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes through diverse and historically significant landscapes. You'll encounter fortresses, historic cities, charming medieval towns, monasteries, Romanesque chapels, and watchtowers. Specific highlights on this section include Berlanga de Duero, the Church of San Martín, Aguilera, and the Collegiate Church of St. Mary of the Market.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this section of El Destierro Route?
faqs.answer: This specific komoot tour starts and ends in Quintanas de Gormaz, near Berlanga de Duero. As stages typically end in towns with amenities, parking should be available in or near the starting village. We recommend checking local parking options upon arrival.

faqs.question: Is El Destierro Route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route itself traverses public lands and roads, specific rules for dogs can vary by local municipality or protected natural areas you might pass through. It's generally advisable to keep dogs on a leash, especially near livestock or in populated areas. Always ensure your dog is well-behaved and that you clean up after them.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for El Destierro Route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle the El Destierro Route itself, as it primarily uses public roads and tracks. However, some specific attractions or natural parks along the broader Camino del Cid might have their own entry requirements or fees, so it's good to check if you plan to visit those.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers significant sections of the El Destierro trail (65%) and also includes parts of the GR 160 - Camino del Cid BTT (11%) and the Camino del Cid (Senderista) (8%).

faqs.question: What kind of bike is suitable for this route?
faqs.answer: For the road cycling route, any cycle touring bike is suitable, though avoiding particularly narrow tires is recommended for slightly uneven sections. For the gravel/MTB route, a gravel bike with good tires is sufficient, or a mountain bike for more challenging sections.

faqs.question: Are there amenities or services available along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the El Destierro Route is designed so that all stages typically end in towns with amenities. This means you'll find convenient stops for food, water, and accommodation, making it well-suited for multi-day cycle touring.

waypoints.name: Collegiate Church of St. Mary of the Market
